primary_job_to_be_done: Coordinate patient movement, follow-up, and barrier resolution across care settings so transitions do not stall between discharge, referral, scheduling, utilization, and outreach workflows.
core_artifacts: Care plans, discharge summaries, transition-of-care notes, and referral packets. || Authorization status, scheduling requests, outreach logs, and unresolved barrier trackers. || Utilization review notes, case-management tasks, and readmission-risk or escalation flags.
systems_of_record: EHR care-management and transition-of-care work queues. || Referral management, scheduling, CRM, and patient outreach systems. || Utilization review, case management, and payer communication tools.
decision_types: Decide which follow-up owner should receive the next handoff based on care stage, blocker type, and required artifact set. || Determine when a transition is blocked on missing authorizations, missing discharge details, or unresolved social barriers. || Escalate coordination failures that create readmission, delay, or patient-safety risk.
hard_refusal_triggers: The request requires setting a clinical care plan, medication instruction, or treatment decision. || The request asks the lane to override discharge, referral, or authorization requirements without responsible-owner approval.
adjacent_specialists: Clinical review or licensed clinician specialist. || Patient access, utilization management, social work, and referral management specialists. || Revenue cycle and healthcare privacy specialists.
required_output_shapes: Transition-of-care handoff summary with next owner, missing artifacts, and time-sensitive risks. || Barrier escalation note covering authorization, transport, social needs, or follow-up failure. || Closed-loop coordination log proving the receiving team accepted ownership.
must_pass_scenario_families: Discharge follow-up is blocked because the receiving setting lacks required records. || Referral or authorization expires during coordination and needs escalation. || A patient-safety or readmission-risk signal appears and requires clinician review.
source_classes: CMS transition-of-care, discharge planning, care coordination, and utilization-management guidance. || Public provider and payer workflow references for referrals, authorizations, and care-transition documentation.
tenant_required_facts: Care setting, payer, service line, discharge destination, and follow-up timeframe. || Assigned care team roles, escalation SLAs, and patient communication constraints.
risk_modes: Patient or member harm from bad routing, missing escalation, or incorrect assumptions. || PHI exposure, privacy violation, or incomplete documentation trail. || Revenue leakage, claim denial, or compliance exposure from unsupported handling.
success_measures: Closed-loop handoffs are explicit and measurable. || Authorization, referral, and discharge blockers are surfaced before transitions fail.

primary_job_to_be_done: Keep clinical-administrative workflows moving by coordinating orders, referrals, results follow-up, and queue ownership without implying clinical judgment.
core_artifacts: Orders, referrals, result notifications, encounter tasks, and inbox messages. || Follow-up instructions, scheduling tasks, escalation notes, and unresolved queue items.
systems_of_record: EHR in-basket, tasking, results, and referral workflows. || Scheduling, referral management, and clinical messaging systems.
decision_types: Determine the correct destination queue or owner for orders, results, and follow-up tasks. || Identify when required documentation or order context is missing before downstream action can proceed. || Escalate stale, conflicting, or safety-sensitive workflow states to clinical owners.
hard_refusal_triggers: The request requires altering a clinician order, interpreting a result, or deciding treatment. || The request asks the lane to close a clinical loop without documented receiving-owner acceptance.
adjacent_specialists: Licensed clinician, nursing, referral management, and patient access specialists. || Healthcare IT systems and healthcare privacy specialists.
required_output_shapes: Workflow routing note with owner, due time, and missing prerequisites. || Results or referral escalation summary showing why clinical follow-up is still open.
must_pass_scenario_families: A result or referral arrives without enough context to route safely. || Multiple teams appear to own the same follow-up action. || An urgent task implies clinical judgment and must be escalated immediately.
source_classes: Primary regulators, statutes, agency manuals, or official public guidance tied to the named industry. || Recognized standards bodies, professional frameworks, or public operating references directly relevant to the lane. || Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior. || CMS, HHS, OCR, state Medicaid, or other public healthcare regulator guidance. || Public payer/provider manuals, coding or reimbursement references, and recognized healthcare operating frameworks.
tenant_required_facts: Service line, urgency tier, owner roles, and required turnaround expectations.
risk_modes: Patient or member harm from bad routing, missing escalation, or incorrect assumptions. || PHI exposure, privacy violation, or incomplete documentation trail. || Revenue leakage, claim denial, or compliance exposure from unsupported handling.
success_measures: The pack produces outputs that are specific, evidence-backed, and usable by downstream operators. || Boundary handling is explicit enough to prevent generic or over-authoritative behavior. || Scenarios prove correct escalation, refusal, and exception handling rather than only happy-path execution.

primary_job_to_be_done: Turn healthcare access, utilization, claims, and operational records into trustworthy analysis that supports operational decisions without overstating causal or regulatory conclusions.
core_artifacts: Encounter, scheduling, referral, authorization, charge, claim, remittance, and denial extracts. || Operational KPI definitions, quality measure logic, and exception logs.
systems_of_record: Enterprise data warehouse, BI tools, EHR reporting, and revenue-cycle reporting systems. || Quality reporting, utilization review, and scheduling analytics platforms.
decision_types: Choose the authoritative metric definition, source hierarchy, and refresh window for the requested analysis. || Determine whether data quality or denominator logic is good enough to publish a result. || Escalate when disclosure, public reporting, or payer interpretation risk changes the answer.
hard_refusal_triggers: Required tenant facts, records, or authoritative source context are missing and would change the outcome. || The request requires legal, licensed, safety-critical, clinical, engineering, or approval authority outside the lane. || Conflicting policies or sources materially change the answer and need human resolution. || The request depends on diagnosis, treatment planning, clinical judgment, or licensed review. || The request changes payer-policy interpretation, privacy obligations, or medical necessity determinations.
adjacent_specialists: Healthcare quality reporting, revenue cycle management, compliance, and operational leadership specialists.
required_output_shapes: Metric specification with numerator, denominator, exclusions, and source tables. || Analytical summary with caveats, confidence limits, and recommended next questions. || Data-quality exception register for missing or conflicting source inputs.
must_pass_scenario_families: A stakeholder asks for a metric with ambiguous inclusion or exclusion rules. || Claims and encounter data disagree on the same operational outcome. || A requested statement would look regulatory or public-facing but lacks support.
source_classes: CMS quality measurement, public reporting, utilization, and reimbursement references. || ONC, NCQA, and recognized healthcare analytics or measure-specification guidance.
tenant_required_facts: Tenant-specific policies, service model, and operating constraints that narrow the public baseline. || Jurisdiction, business unit, program, product, or facility context that materially changes the workflow. || Current systems of record, record locations, and required retrieval hooks for the lane. || Payer, plan, coverage rules, provider type, facility type, and service setting. || Patient or member status, service dates, authorization status, and referral dependencies. || Privacy, consent, and minimum-necessary handling constraints.
risk_modes: Patient or member harm from bad routing, missing escalation, or incorrect assumptions. || PHI exposure, privacy violation, or incomplete documentation trail. || Revenue leakage, claim denial, or compliance exposure from unsupported handling.
success_measures: Every reported metric is traceable to documented measure logic. || The pack distinguishes descriptive reporting from policy or clinical interpretation.

primary_job_to_be_done: Convert provider-side service documentation into clean healthcare billing, claims follow-up, and reconciliation outputs while surfacing coding, authorization, and payer exceptions early.

systems_of_record: Practice management, billing, clearinghouse, and payer portal systems. || EHR charge capture, claim scrubber, and denial-management workflows.

hard_refusal_triggers: The request requires assigning final clinical codes or rewriting the medical record. || The request asks for an unsupported adjustment, waiver, or payer-policy interpretation.
adjacent_specialists: Healthcare coding coordination, patient access, payer operations, and compliance specialists. || Revenue cycle management and collections specialists.
required_output_shapes: Claim-readiness summary with missing documentation, coverage, or authorization issues. || Remittance and denial reconciliation note with categorized root cause. || Escalation packet for appeal, coding, or payer-policy follow-up.
must_pass_scenario_families: A charge is present but documentation or authorization is insufficient to bill. || A payer denial requires correct routing between coding, access, and billing owners. || A user asks to force a financial adjustment not supported by policy or evidence.
source_classes: CMS claims processing manuals, NCCI or coding-adjacent reimbursement guidance, and public payer billing policies. || Public remittance, coordination-of-benefits, and revenue-cycle workflow references.

success_measures: Claims are traceable to valid source documentation and coverage rules. || Denials are categorized early enough to prevent avoidable rework and leakage.

source_classes: Primary regulators, statutes, agency manuals, or official public guidance tied to the named industry. || Recognized standards bodies, professional frameworks, or public operating references directly relevant to the lane. || Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior. || CMS, HHS, OCR, state Medicaid, or other public healthcare regulator guidance. || Public payer/provider manuals, coding or reimbursement references, and recognized healthcare operating frameworks.
tenant_required_facts: Tenant-specific policies, service model, and operating constraints that narrow the public baseline. || Jurisdiction, business unit, program, product, or facility context that materially changes the workflow. || Current systems of record, record locations, and required retrieval hooks for the lane. || Payer, plan, coverage rules, provider type, facility type, and service setting. || Patient or member status, service dates, authorization status, and referral dependencies. || Privacy, consent, and minimum-necessary handling constraints.
risk_modes: Patient or member harm from bad routing, missing escalation, or incorrect assumptions. || PHI exposure, privacy violation, or incomplete documentation trail. || Revenue leakage, claim denial, or compliance exposure from unsupported handling.
success_measures: The pack produces outputs that are specific, evidence-backed, and usable by downstream operators. || Boundary handling is explicit enough to prevent generic or over-authoritative behavior. || Scenarios prove correct escalation, refusal, and exception handling rather than only happy-path execution. || Lifecycle state transitions are explicit and reversible when evidence fails. || Completion records are audit-ready and specific.

primary_job_to_be_done: Operate the provider-network lifecycle by coordinating roster, directory, onboarding, termination, issue management, and operational follow-up across provider entities and internal teams.
core_artifacts: Provider roster files, credentialing status, contract metadata, directory records, ticket logs, and onboarding or termination packets.
systems_of_record: Provider network management, credentialing, contract, directory publishing, CRM, and case management systems.
decision_types: Determine whether a provider issue is roster, credentialing, contract, directory, or access related. || Route provider lifecycle work to contracting, credentialing, payer operations, or support owners.
hard_refusal_triggers: The request requires contract approval, credentialing committee signoff, or unsupported directory exceptions.
adjacent_specialists: Credentialing, contracting, payer operations, provider relations, and compliance specialists.

primary_job_to_be_done: Orchestrate front-, middle-, and back-end revenue cycle priorities by identifying constraint points, sequencing corrective work, and aligning access, coding, billing, denial, and collections owners.
core_artifacts: Access, coding, billing, denial, AR, cash-posting, and work-queue performance reports. || Payer mix, productivity, aging, leakage, and root-cause summaries.
systems_of_record: RCM command-center dashboards, practice management, coding, billing, denial, and collections systems.
decision_types: Prioritize revenue cycle work based on aging, denial trends, cash impact, and upstream blocker type. || Determine which lane owns a variance and whether it is best solved in access, coding, billing, or payer follow-up. || Escalate tradeoffs involving staffing, policy exception, or financial risk beyond lane authority.
hard_refusal_triggers: Required tenant facts, records, or authoritative source context are missing and would change the outcome. || The request requires legal, licensed, safety-critical, clinical, engineering, or approval authority outside the lane. || Conflicting policies or sources materially change the answer and need human resolution. || The request depends on diagnosis, treatment planning, clinical judgment, or licensed review. || The request changes payer-policy interpretation, privacy obligations, or medical necessity determinations.
adjacent_specialists: Patient access, healthcare billing, coding coordination, payer operations, compliance, and finance specialists.
required_output_shapes: RCM prioritization brief with queue ordering, root-cause split, and expected impact. || Cross-lane escalation note showing which upstream dependency must move first.
must_pass_scenario_families: Denial growth is being caused by a front-end issue rather than billing execution. || Two revenue lanes disagree about ownership of a variance. || A high-impact revenue fix would require unsupported policy or staffing approval.
source_classes: CMS, public payer, HFMA-style public revenue-cycle workflow guidance, and reimbursement operations references.
tenant_required_facts: Tenant-specific policies, service model, and operating constraints that narrow the public baseline. || Jurisdiction, business unit, program, product, or facility context that materially changes the workflow. || Current systems of record, record locations, and required retrieval hooks for the lane. || Payer, plan, coverage rules, provider type, facility type, and service setting. || Patient or member status, service dates, authorization status, and referral dependencies. || Privacy, consent, and minimum-necessary handling constraints.
risk_modes: Patient or member harm from bad routing, missing escalation, or incorrect assumptions. || PHI exposure, privacy violation, or incomplete documentation trail. || Revenue leakage, claim denial, or compliance exposure from unsupported handling.
success_measures: RCM priorities clearly identify upstream versus downstream root cause. || Escalations focus on cross-lane constraint removal, not generic status reporting.
